description: An object is 'phelo' when its low physical weight requires little force to lift or support in context. It is the counterpart of 'tumoa' (heavy), while 'pamolu' names weight before either judgement. Illumination belongs to 'keru' (bright), and Phi states freedom from obligation through its burden vocabulary rather than the English homonym 'light'.
articulatory_notes: The lips narrow without closing for voiceless bilabial 'ph' and release into true-mid front 'e'. The tongue tip then contacts the alveolar ridge for 'l' before rounded mid-back 'o'. Stress falls on 'phe'.
sound_symbolism: Breath passes through 'ph' and continues around the tongue at 'l' without a full closure. That easy passage offers a mnemonic for low weight.
usage_notes: The phrase 'phelo wiru' means a light basket. Any object may take 'phelo' when its physical weight is at issue. Comparison or measurement of the neutral magnitude takes 'pamolu' without calling it high or low.

semantic_domains.physical: low physical weight relative to the force needed for lifting or support
